draft.introduction: The Ayodhya Kanda narrates Rama’s exile—the moment when the ideal prince accepts banishment to the forest for fourteen years to honor his father’s promise. Dharma is chosen over comfort.

translation.text: Dasharatha resolved to consecrate Rama as heir apparent. The city rejoiced, for Rama was beloved of all. But Kaikeyi, reminded by the hunchbacked Manthara, invoked two boons promised long ago by the king.

Rama accepted exile without hesitation: “I shall go to the forest and dwell there for fourteen years, wearing bark and matted hair, so that my father’s word may be honored.” Lakshmana and Sita chose to follow him. The city of Ayodhya wept as its prince departed.

translator_notes.note: Rama’s acceptance of exile exemplifies the Hindu concept of dharma as duty that transcends personal desire. His willingness to suffer injustice rather than violate his father’s word makes him the model of righteous conduct in Indian civilization.
